So here's a question for you to chew on. What small thing could have the big impact of making a person four times more confident in their ability to guess how many gumballs are in a giant jar? Some researchers actually studied this strange task, and their findings help illustrate the power of commitment and consistency. The researchers set up a contest in a shopping mall where people could pay $10 to submit a guess as to how many gumballs were in an enormous jar. The most accurate guess would get $20,000, an incredible prize. But it wasn't really people's gumball guesses that the researchers were interested in. Instead, they wanted to know how sure people were that their guesses would be correct. The researchers discovered something fascinating by asking people about how confident they were both just before they made a guess and then just after they made that guess. What did they find? Right after paying $10 to enter their guess into the contest, the average person reported being four times more confident about their chances of winning the prize relative to how confident they felt just before writing down their guess. So what happened to the shoppers in the seconds it took to officially submit their guess and place their $10 on the line? The answer is that they had made a commitment. They had committed $10 to a number. This illustrates the power of commitment and consistency. What's people have made a choice, taken a stand or adopted a position? They feel tremendous personal and interpersonal pressure to act in ways that are consistent with it.
